notes: Heuristic reference priors for sports-related prediction markets. These are not betting edges or guarantees. Use only as lightweight starting points before adjusting for current information and clear market resolution rules.

default.notes: Quarterback quality and injury news matter more than most single-player impacts in team sports.

heavy_favorite.notes: Use only when team quality, quarterback edge, and injury status all support the gap.

balanced_match.notes: Useful for division games or uncertain injury situations.

default.notes: Draws are generally not relevant for standard moneyline markets. Rest, injuries, and schedule are major inputs.

heavy_favorite.notes: Use when roster strength and schedule context clearly favor one side.

balanced_match.notes: Useful when teams are close in quality or uncertainty is high.

default.notes: Baseball moneylines are often tighter because single-game variance is high.

heavy_favorite.notes: Use when the pitching and lineup edge is clearly one-sided.

balanced_match.notes: Useful when pitching matchups are close or lineups are uncertain.

default.notes: Goalie status and one-game variance are large drivers.

heavy_favorite.notes: Use only when goalie, rest, and roster news all align.

balanced_match.notes: Useful for closely matched teams or volatile spots.

default.notes: Soccer has relatively high draw frequency. Late injuries, lineup rotation, and red-card risk matter a lot.

heavy_favorite.notes: Use only when the favorite is materially stronger and lineup/news support the gap.

balanced_match.notes: Useful for derby, knockout, or otherwise close matches.

default.notes: Surface fit, recent form, fatigue, and retirement risk are key.

heavy_favorite.notes: Use only when ranking gap, surface fit, and health all support it.

balanced_match.notes: Useful for near pick'em matches.

default.notes: Format matters a lot: T20 is more volatile than longer formats.

heavy_favorite.notes: Use when squad quality and venue conditions clearly favor one side.

balanced_match.notes: Useful when toss/conditions could swing the game.

default.notes: Golf outrights are high-variance; head-to-head markets are usually more forecastable.

heavy_favorite.notes: Use when the market is a matchup or tightly defined prop, not a large-field outright unless resolution is very specific.

balanced_match.notes: Useful for close head-to-head golf matchups.

default.notes: Qualifying and car/team pace drive much of the baseline in race winner or head-to-head markets.

heavy_favorite.notes: Use only when pace advantage and starting position strongly align.

balanced_match.notes: Useful for teammate head-to-heads or uncertain strategy spots.

default.notes: Use for non-F1 motorsport markets where pace, grid, and reliability matter heavily.

heavy_favorite.notes: Use when one driver/rider or team has a pronounced equipment and pace edge.

balanced_match.notes: Useful for tighter head-to-head or parity-heavy series.

default.notes: Style matchup and short-notice changes can matter more than raw record.

heavy_favorite.notes: Use only when grappling/striking edge and durability profile strongly support it.

balanced_match.notes: Useful for volatile matchups with real finish equity on both sides.

default.notes: Judging variance and inactivity can distort apparently clean priors.

heavy_favorite.notes: Use when class difference and recent form are clear.

balanced_match.notes: Useful for closer championship or veteran matchups.

default.notes: Home field and squad rotation often matter materially.

heavy_favorite.notes: Use when depth and physical mismatch are clear.

balanced_match.notes: Useful for derby or tournament spots.

default.notes: Singles and doubles have different dynamics, so use caution with generic priors.

heavy_favorite.notes: Use when ranking/form gap is obvious and fitness is known.

balanced_match.notes: Useful for evenly matched tour-level contests.

default.notes: Use caution when moving between indoor and beach formats.

heavy_favorite.notes: Use when serve/receive or depth edge is meaningful.

balanced_match.notes: Useful for closely rated teams or pairs.

default.notes: Fast-match formats can be noisy; style edges matter a lot.

heavy_favorite.notes: Use when class gap is obvious and recent form is stable.

balanced_match.notes: Useful for close pairings or volatile tournament stages.

default.notes: Home-court and depth can create strong edges.

heavy_favorite.notes: Use when squad quality and venue edge clearly favor one side.

balanced_match.notes: Useful for tighter continental matchups.

default.notes: Shorter formats increase volatility materially.

heavy_favorite.notes: Use when scoring floor and recent form strongly support the favorite.

balanced_match.notes: Useful for close seeded players or shorter formats.

default.notes: Cycling markets are highly context-sensitive; route type matters enormously.

heavy_favorite.notes: Use when team support and route fit clearly favor one rider.

balanced_match.notes: Useful for deep fields or uncertain race dynamics.

default.notes: Longer formats often favor the stronger player more than short formats.

heavy_favorite.notes: Use when long-format class difference is clear.

balanced_match.notes: Useful for close ranking matchups or shorter-format events.

default.notes: Roster changes, map pool, patch effects, and online/offline format can swing outcomes quickly.

heavy_favorite.notes: Use when team quality and current form clearly diverge.

balanced_match.notes: Useful when teams are close or volatility is elevated.
